data.title: Collegium raises 2025 revenue guidance to $760M as Jornay PM growth accelerates
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Collegium Pharmaceutical (COLL) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Vikram Karnani, President and CEO, reported, "We generated both top and bottom line growth in the quarter, including record revenue from Jornay and returned value to our shareholders through our share repurchases." Karnani highlighted strategic priorities: driving growth for Jornay PM, maximizing the pain portfolio's durability, and deploying capital to enhance shareholder value.
  • Karnani stated, "During our third full quarter of owning Jornay, prescriptions grew 23% year-over-year, and we generated record quarterly revenues of $32.6 million. We also generated another quarter of meaningful growth from our pain portfolio with a record $155.4 million in combined revenues, up 7% year-over-year."
  • Karnani announced, "Our Board recently approved a new $150 million share repurchase program authorized through December 2026."
  • Karnani also confirmed leadership updates: "Gino Santini, our Lead Independent Director, was appointed as Chairman of the Board. Additionally, Dr. Carlos Paya was elected by our shareholders to join the Board following his nomination last quarter."
  • Karnani raised 2025 financial guidance, stating, "We now expect to grow total revenue by approximately 19% year-over-year... We now expect Jornay revenue to be in the range of $140 million to $145 million, representing roughly 42% growth from 2024 pro forma revenue."
  • Scott Dreyer, Chief Commercial Officer, emphasized, "Jornay's market share of the long-acting branded methylphenidate market grew to 23%, up 7.6 percentage points year-over-year. Jornay has a broad and growing prescriber base, reaching an all-time high of over 26,000 prescribers in the second quarter, up 23%."
  • Dreyer noted, "In April, we completed the expansion of our sales force, adding approximately 55 new representatives, bringing the total ADHD sales force to approximately 180 representatives."
  • Colleen Tupper, CFO, stated, "We delivered strong financial results in the second quarter, growing revenue 29% and adjusted EBITDA 9% year-over-year, while making targeted investments to drive continued growth in Jornay PM."

Outlook

  • Colleen Tupper reported, "We now expect total product revenues in the range of $745 million to $760 million, a $10 million increase compared to our prior guidance range, resulting in a 19% growth year-over-year."
  • Tupper provided further detail: "We now expect Jornay revenue to be in the range of $140 million to $145 million in 2025 compared to our prior guidance of at least $135 million."
  • Adjusted EBITDA guidance was raised to a range of $440 million to $455 million, a $5 million increase from the prior range and a 12% increase year-over-year.
  • Adjusted operating expenses are expected in the range of $225 million to $235 million, a $5 million increase from the prior guidance range.
  • Management expects quarterly adjusted operating expenses to trend down in the second half of the year.

Financial Results

  • Tupper reported, "Net product revenues were $188 million, up 29% year-over-year. Jornay net revenue was $32.6 million. Belbuca net revenue was $52.6 million, up 1% year-over-year. Xtampza net revenue was $52.6 million, up 18% year-over-year. Nucynta Franchise net revenue was $46.4 million, up 4% year-over-year."
  • GAAP operating expenses were $73.3 million, up 69% year-over-year. Non-GAAP adjusted operating expenses were $61.9 million, up 104% year-over-year.
  • GAAP net income was $12 million compared to $19.6 million in Q2 2024.
  • Non-GAAP adjusted EBITDA was $105.1 million, up 9% year-over-year.
  • GAAP earnings per share was $0.38 basic, $0.34 diluted; non-GAAP adjusted EPS was $1.68.
  • The company generated $72.4 million in cash from operations and ended the quarter with $222.2 million in cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities.

Q&A

  • Leszek Sulewski, Truist Securities: Asked about the target number of additional prescribers for Jornay PM, awareness levels, progress in the adult ADHD market, and whether the $300 million to $400 million peak sales estimate for Jornay PM will be revisited. Vikram Karnani responded that the peak sales guidance will be revisited after assessing expanded sales performance. Scott Dreyer explained, "What we're focused on is continued breadth of prescribing...26,000 this quarter, that's up 23% year-over-year. There's no sign of slowing down." Dreyer also highlighted, "Already Jornay has 20% of its business with adults...the growth by type is this. We grew 21% in [indiscernible]. We grew 33% in adult."
  • Sulewski followed up on investments in the pain portfolio. Dreyer noted, "We have about 100 salespeople...Every year as part of brand planning, we check in on targets and what we need from an investment standpoint, and that's consistent."
  • John Todaro, Needham & Company: Asked about specific levers for top-line growth in Q3 and developments in generic competition for the pain portfolio. Dreyer highlighted, "The expansion is going really great. We're seeing great early indicators...we saw 2,700 of those new targets wrote a prescription." Tupper provided, "There is no party that has the necessary combination of all the ingredients needed to launch...competitive generics against any product in our pain portfolio."
  • David A. Amsellem, Piper Sandler: Inquired about the types of assets targeted for M&A or BD beyond pain/CNS, and whether the company would consider noncommercial or late-stage assets. Karnani replied, "We remain very committed to being disciplined in our approach...to the extent that we can leverage our investments and our infrastructure that is already in place, that would be our ideal target from a BD perspective."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed for clarification on Jornay's peak sales potential, prescriber growth, and business development strategy, reflecting a neutral to slightly positive tone as they sought confirmation of the sustainability of current trends rather than expressing skepticism or concern.
  • Management maintained a confident and positive tone throughout, frequently referencing strong commercial performance and financial flexibility. Quotes such as "We are well positioned for our next phase of growth, and I am confident in our ability to deliver on our financial and strategic commitments" (Karnani) and "We're pleased with the initial progress and expect to see additional benefits as we head into the second half of the year and 2026" (Dreyer) illustrate this.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts' tone remains generally confident, with management slightly more assertive due to upgraded guidance and analysts focused on confirmation of execution and long-term prospects.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company raised its 2025 revenue guidance to $745 million–$760 million from the previous $735 million–$750 million, and Jornay PM's guidance to $140 million–$145 million from at least $135 million previously, reflecting increasing confidence in growth drivers.
  • Adjusted EBITDA guidance was lifted to $440 million–$455 million from $435 million–$450 million.
  • Jornay PM prescriptions and prescriber base increased, with market share now at 23%, compared to 20.3% in Q1.
  • Management tone shifted from cautious optimism in Q1 to more confident assertions in Q2, underpinned by record revenues and expanded commercial investments.
  • Analysts continued to focus on key growth metrics for Jornay and generic risk for the pain portfolio, but with greater emphasis on the realization of recent investments.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified increased operating expenses, primarily due to investments in Jornay commercialization and sales force expansion, as a near-term cost pressure, but expects expenses to trend down in the second half.
  • On generic competition, Tupper stated, "There is no party that has the necessary combination of all the ingredients needed to launch...competitive generics against any product in our pain portfolio."
  • Analysts focused on the durability of the pain franchise and the path to expanded prescriber growth for Jornay, with management reiterating confidence in both areas.

Final Takeaway

Collegium Pharmaceutical delivered record revenue and raised its 2025 guidance, driven by accelerating growth in Jornay PM and consistent performance across its pain portfolio. The company expanded its sales force, launched new marketing campaigns, and authorized a $150 million share repurchase program, all while maintaining strong cash flow and a solid balance sheet. Management remains confident in the durability of its core franchises and committed to executing its strategy for growth and shareholder value.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: GM to import CATL batteries for Chevy Bolt despite China tariffs
data.description:
General Motors (NYSE:GM) will reportedly import batteries from Contemporary Amperex Technology (CATL) for the next two years despite steep tariffs imposed by Washington on imports from China, The Wall Street Journal reported.

The batteries will be used in the new Chevy Bolt, which will begin production at GM’s (NYSE:GM) Fairfax, Kansas production facility in late 2025.

The company said the decision to buy batteries from CATL – and absorb an additional cost of as much as 80% -- is a “stopgap” until its joint venture with LG Energy Solutions can begin commercial production of lower cost lithium iron phosphate (LFP) batteries in 2027.

“For several years, other U.S. automakers have depended on foreign suppliers for LFP battery sourcing and licensing,” a GM spokesman told the Wall Street Journal. “To stay competitive, GM will temporarily source these packs from similar suppliers to power our most affordable EV model.”

With the $7,500 EV tax credit no longer in play, GM’s decision to use CATL batteries is less burdensome, as Chinese-made batteries would have previously made the Bolt significantly more expensive—and less competitive—compared to EVs that qualified for the credit.

data.title: Should Eli Lilly slide on data for obesity pill despite Q2 beat?
data.description:
Eli Lilly (NYSE:LLY) shares reached a new 52-week low on Thursday as concerns over late-stage trial data for the company’s highly anticipated oral weight loss therapy, orforglipron, overshadowed its better-than-expected Q2 2025 financials.

Citing initial results, the Indiana-based pharmaceutical giant said that the once-daily GLP-1 receptor agonist caused an average weight loss of 12.4% over 72 weeks at the highest dose, reaching the main goals in its Phase 3 ATTAIN-1 trial.

Novo Nordisk (NVO), whose oral weight loss candidate amycretin caused up to 13% weight loss over 12 weeks in a Phase 1 trial, added ~7% in reaction despite a recent decision to lower its growth outlook based on competitive headwinds for its GLP-1 franchise.

Leerink Partners was quick to downgrade LLY after orforglipron data as analyst David Risinger lowered long-term projections for the drug, citing concerns over its efficacy and safety.

Analyst argued that orforglipron has underperformed despite his initial expectations that it could lead to about 17% weight loss, the level of efficacy Novo's (NVO) obesity drug semaglutide showed at 2.4mg in a Phase 3 trial called STEP-1.

However, Risinger welcomed orforglipron’s tolerability profile, which he said was in line with his initial expectations despite a slightly higher rate of discontinuations than seen in Phase 3 data for semaglutide.

Leerink downgraded LLY to Market Perform from Outperform and lowered its price target to $715 from $944 per share, slashing its 2030 sales estimates for orforglipron by ~38% to $13.5B.

Despite LLY’s Q2 beat and guidance raise, Risinger pointed to competitive headwinds in the GLP-1 market, particularly pricing pressure from NVO and lack of healthcare coverage for anti-obesity medications, as his other reasons for the downgrade.

However, Bank of America defended Lilly (NYSE:LLY), noting that the selloff was overdone, as data for orforglipron represented only a topline readout, and “there are four more Phase 3 trials coming over the next five months?”

“Compared to Novo’s troubled Q2,” Eli Lilly’s (NYSE:LLY) print looked “refreshing,” with no major surprises, analyst Tim Anderson wrote, maintaining his Buy rating and $1,000 per share target.

data.title: Royal Gold outlines expanded portfolio diversification and maintains 2025 guidance as acquisitions advance
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Royal Gold (RGLD) Q2 2025

Management View

  • President and CEO William H. Heissenbuttel opened the call emphasizing, “Our second quarter was another excellent quarter for Royal Gold with new records for revenue, earnings and cash flow.” He cited “earnings for the quarter were $132 million or $2.01 per share,” and highlighted record adjusted results after discrete tax items: “After adjusting for these items, earnings were a strong $119 million or $1.81 per share.”
  • Heissenbuttel stated that gold contributed about 78% of total revenue and attributed the company’s “84%” adjusted EBITDA margin to “the strong gold price, combined with our low and stable cash G&A.”
  • Heissenbuttel announced the achievement of the “full offset of the Pueblo Viejo advanced stream deposit” and indicated expectations for continued revenue from this asset into the mid-2040s.
  • On strategy, Heissenbuttel described the Sandstorm Gold and Horizon Copper acquisitions as “significantly add[ing] to our scale, growth and diversification,” projecting “sector-leading asset diversification with no asset accounting for more than about 12% of NAV.” He added, “These transactions do not shift our strategic focus. We remain focused on growth in precious metals, maintaining a strong balance sheet and liquidity and increasing our dividend.”
  • Heissenbuttel detailed three recent asset additions: the gold stream on Kansanshi mine in Zambia, a stream and royalty interest on the Warintza project in Ecuador, and a royalty on the Lawyers-Ranch project in British Columbia.
  • Heissenbuttel welcomed Mark Isto to the Board, citing his “institutional knowledge with Royal Gold and broad technical background.”
  • Martin Raffield, Senior Vice President of Operations, reported “overall revenue was a record $210 million with volume of 63,900 GEOs,” and highlighted “royalty revenue was up by about 50% from the prior year quarter to $77 million.”
  • Paul K. Libner, CFO, stated, “Revenue for the quarter was up strongly by 20% to $210 million, which was another record for the company.” He added, “Net income for the quarter increased significantly over the prior year to a record $132.3 million or $2.01 per share.”

Outlook

  • Management confirmed they are “maintaining our 2025 guidance ranges for metal sales, DD&A and tax rate.” Libner stated, “We are maintaining our 2025 guidance ranges for metal sales, DD&A and tax rate.”
  • Raffield provided an update that “Centerra reported yesterday that they had encountered lower-than-expected gold grades… and reduce[d] their 2025 gold production guidance to 145,000 to 165,000 ounces.” He noted, “There is no change to the previous copper production guidance of 50 million to 60 million pounds.”
  • Management reiterated the expectation of delivery timing for deferred gold consideration from Mount Milligan in the latter part of Q3 or early Q4.

Financial Results

  • Libner reported, “Operating cash flow this quarter was also a record at $153 million, up significantly from $114 million in the prior period.”
  • G&A expense was “$10.3 million and was in line with the prior year.”
  • The company “remained debt-free at the end of the quarter,” and “total liquidity grew to just over $1.25 billion.”
  • The company drew “$825 million on the revolver and used $175 million of our available cash to fund the [Kansanshi] acquisition,” with an “all-in borrowing rate on the recent draw… approximately 5.5%.”

Q&A

  • Fahad Tariq, Jefferies, asked about deleveraging post-transaction. Heissenbuttel responded, “The plan, as it always is, is to take excess cash flow each quarter and pay down the revolver. And at some point, we might get it back to 0.”
  • Lawson Winder, BofA, questioned guidance resilience despite asset underperformance. Raffield explained, “We do carry out a rigorous risk-adjusted budgeting and guidance preparation process… we are comfortable with maintaining our 2025 guidance range at this stage in the year, even though those have been offset somewhat by Milligan underperformance in Xavantina guidance reduction.”
  • Winder asked about further African exposure; Heissenbuttel replied, “It’s not so much a continent approach that we would take. We’re very comfortable in the 3 countries where we have interest.”
  • Winder queried a potential buyback amid share underperformance. Heissenbuttel answered, “At this point, I don’t think so. What we’re going to do with excess cash flow at this point is pay down the debt.”
  • Joshua Mark Wolfson, RBC, sought details on Sandstorm transaction timing. Heissenbuttel stated, “We’re going to shortly file the preliminary proxy with the SEC… there’s nothing we see that makes us think the fourth quarter isn’t a good target.”
  • Wolfson asked about technical filings; Raffield responded, “We’re not planning on filing any documents for the -- or any 43-101 documents for these properties at the moment, no.”
  • Wolfson requested clarity on accounting for deferred gold ounces. Libner clarified, “We have no basis in those ounces yet. So once we receive those ounces… the deferred liability will go up by the fair market value of those ounces received.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised pointed questions about deleveraging, exposure to higher-risk jurisdictions, and share price underperformance, expressing a slightly negative to neutral sentiment.
  • Management maintained a confident and consistent tone, emphasizing a disciplined approach to cash flow, risk, and guidance: “We are comfortable with maintaining our 2025 guidance range at this stage in the year.”
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst tone shifted to more concern regarding growth visibility, risk diversification, and capital allocation, while management remained confident but more frequently addressed risk and strategic questions.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q2 saw new record highs in revenue, earnings, and operating cash flow, surpassing Q1’s previous records.
  • The company advanced its strategic agenda with the Sandstorm and Horizon acquisitions, compared to a prior quarter focused primarily on incremental asset additions.
  • Management maintained guidance and their focus on precious metals, but the discussion in Q2 included more detail on portfolio diversification and acquisition-driven growth.
  • Analysts in Q2 focused more on capital allocation, deleveraging, share performance, and jurisdictional risk, in contrast to the Q1 focus on tax guidance, quarterly production phasing, and asset-specific opportunities.
  • Management’s confidence in guidance and capital discipline remained steady, but the scope of responses broadened to address new strategic and risk-related topics.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged lower-than-expected gold grades at Mount Milligan and a subsequent reduction in operator guidance, but expressed confidence in portfolio risk adjustment processes.
  • Asset-specific issues such as the temporary shutdown at Andacollo and guidance reductions at Xavantina were discussed, with management noting the diversified portfolio’s ability to offset underperformance.
  • Analysts raised concerns over increased African exposure and potential political volatility; management emphasized geographic diversification and a country-specific approach.
  • No immediate plans for share buybacks were confirmed, with priority given to debt repayment.

Final Takeaway

Royal Gold delivered record financial performance in Q2 2025 while executing on significant strategic acquisitions to expand portfolio scale and diversification. Management underscored their intent to maintain a precious metals focus, disciplined capital allocation, and a strong balance sheet, with clear confidence in the durability of their 2025 guidance and the long-term cash flow potential of newly acquired and existing assets.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Nasdaq turns red, stocks extend losses after weak bond auction
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages on Thursday turned lower, as positive sentiment sparked by U.S. President Donald Trump's chip tariff exemptions and progress on Russia-Ukraine de-escalation petered out.

A $25B 30-year Treasury bond auction tailed, signaling weak demand for fixed-income securities and further weighing on investors' moods. It was the third straight weak Treasury auction this week.

The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rose 2 basis points to 4.25%, while the 2-year yield (US2Y) was up 2 basis points to 3.74%.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last -0.3% in afternoon trade, giving up an advance of as much as +0.7%. The blue-chip Dow (DJI) was -0.8%. 

The Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) also slipped into negative territory after steadily paring gains. It had earlier been boosted by a post-earnings climb in AppLovin (APP) and DoorDash (DASH), and extended gains in tech titan Apple (AAPL). The iPhone-maker on Wednesday confirmed a new $100B investment in the U.S.

The tech-heavy index was also boosted by several chip stocks, as investors took heart from Trump's vow to exempt semiconductor manufacturers from a planned 100% tariff on imports if they made their products in the United States.

President Trump “won’t charge tariffs while you’re building in the country, but if you’re not building in the country, 100% tariffs,” Secretary of Commerce Howard Lutnick told Fox Business in the morning.

Shares of Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM), the world's largest contract chipmaker, clinched a record high on Thursday after Taiwan said TSM will not have to pay the 100% tariff.

Speaking of tariffs, reciprocal barriers that had been postponed twice finally went into effect against U.S. trading partners across the world on Wednesday, bringing the average tariff rate to levels not seen since World War II.   

“Is this the new global tariff regime? Not quite yet. While some countries are still negotiating with the U.S., America’s new tariff regime entered into effect at midnight, increasing the effective U.S. tariff level from just under 3% at the beginning of the year to just over 15%,” said Mohamed A. El-Erian, chief economic adviser at Allianz and former CEO of PIMCO.

“Up to this point, many companies appear to have used inventories and profit margins as buffers, as well as pressure on suppliers, while waiting to see what would finally emerge. Now, they must make more challenging, longer-term decisions about whether and how much to adjust prices,” El-Erian added. 

Also grabbing headlines were signs of progress on a truce between Russia and Ukraine.

"We had some very good talks with President Putin today, and there's a very good chance that we could be ending the round — the end of that road. There's a good chance that there will be a meeting very soon," Trump told reporters on Wednesday.

Meanwhile, The New York Times reported that Trump was planning to hold in-person meetings with Russian and Ukrainian leaders Vladimir Putin and Volodymyr Zelenskyy as soon as next week. 

Turning to Thursday's economic calendar, initial jobless claims jumped more than expected, while continuing claims rose to their highest mark since 2021.

data.title: Judge vacates Fed's debit-card 'swipe fee' cap rule in win for retailers - report
data.description:
In a ruling favoring retailers, a federal judge vacated a Federal Reserve rule on debit card "swipe fees," saying the regulator went beyond what Congress allowed in laws it passed in 2010, according to media reports on Thursday.

The law only allowed banks to charge retailers interchange fees that would cover incremental costs of processing debit card transactions on card networks such as Visa (V) and Mastercard (MA). The Fed exceeded its authority by adding costs for other things, such as fraud prevention in the interchange fee calculation when it finalized the rules in 2011, Judge Daniel M. Traynor said, according to reporting by Bloomberg Law.

While the payment networks set the fee, the banks and card issuers kept most of the fees. Traynor vacated the rule, but he stayed the decision, pending a potential appeal from the Fed "to prevent interchange transaction fees from becoming a completely unregulated market."

The Fed will be allowed to proceed with a 2023 proposal to reduce the swipe fee cap from the current 21-cent per swipe level to 14.4 cents.

Relevant tickers: Visa (V), Mastercard (MA), JPMorgan Chase (NYSE:JPM), Bank of America (BAC), Citigroup (C), Wells Fargo (WFC), Synchrony Financial (NYSE:SYF), Capital One Financial (NYSE:COF), Bread Financial Holdings (BFH).

data.title: IIPR outlines $270M life science sector move while targeting 7% cannabis market CAGR through 2029
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Innovative Industrial Properties (IIPR) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Alan D. Gold, Executive Chairman, announced a major strategic shift with IIPR’s first expansion outside the cannabis industry via a $270 million investment in IQHQ, a private life science REIT. Gold stated, "This investment underscores our conviction in the long-term fundamentals of the life science industry and provides IIP a unique opportunity to accretively deploy capital while adding industry and tenant diversification to our portfolio and positioning us to continue driving growth and creating long-term value for our shareholders."

  • Gold detailed that IQHQ’s portfolio includes over 5 million square feet in key global life science markets and described the investment as "expected to be highly accretive to AFFO, carrying a blended yield exceeding 14%." The structure includes a $100 million revolving credit facility funding at closing and up to $170 million in preferred stock over time.

  • Paul E. Smithers, CEO, emphasized ongoing commitment to cannabis real estate, highlighting industry growth projections and acknowledging headwinds. Smithers explained, "Although the cannabis industry continues to face challenges, including persistent macroeconomic uncertainty and an unpredictable regulatory backdrop, it is still forecasted to grow at a compounded annual growth rate of approximately 7% from 2024 to 2029, reaching $44 billion by 2029."

  • Smithers also gave a detailed update on tenant legal proceedings, including actions involving 4Front Ventures, Gold Flora, PharmaCann, and TILT Holdings, and described ongoing efforts to optimize occupancy and tenant quality.

  • Ben Regin, Chief Investment Officer, noted, "Year-to-date, we have closed on a $7.8 million acquisition in Maryland, completed two dispositions totaling $10.8 million in Michigan and California and executed two new leases totaling 211,000 square feet also in Michigan and California."

  • CFO David J. Smith stated, "For the second quarter, we generated total revenues of $62.9 million, a 12% decrease from the first quarter of this year. The decrease was primarily driven by the tenant defaults we previously disclosed in March."

Outlook

  • Gold projected that "life science fundraising in 2025 is on track to be its highest since 2021." He described the IQHQ investment as positioning IIPR to "capitalize on these long-term secular tailwinds" in life sciences and indicated that the company will continue to seek investment opportunities in both cannabis and life sciences.

  • Management reiterated focus on optimizing cannabis portfolio occupancy, pursuing legal remedies for defaulted tenants, and leveraging the right of first offer (ROFO) on future IQHQ asset sales for further growth. No updated explicit guidance figures beyond those in prepared remarks.

Financial Results

  • IIPR reported total revenues of $62.9 million for Q2 2025, a 12% sequential decrease, attributed to previously disclosed tenant defaults.

  • Adjusted funds from operations (AFFO) for the quarter was $48.4 million, or $1.71 per share, down 12% from Q1 2025.

  • Smith highlighted a strong balance sheet "backed by $2.6 billion in primarily unencumbered gross assets" and "strong liquidity exceeding $190 million through cash on hand and an undrawn revolver."

  • During the quarter, IIPR repurchased 367,000 shares at a weighted average price of $53.98 for $19.8 million, funded with cash on hand and preferred stock issued in the quarter.

Q&A

  • William Thomas Catherwood, BTIG, questioned the investment case for IQHQ and the timing. Gold explained, "We believe that there is a -- that the industry is -- has an opportunity to recover, and that recovery has begun. And that valuations are still extremely low, are starting to move in a very positive way. As a financial investment, our investment in IQHQ is -- was very well thought out, very well researched."

  • Catherwood asked about IQHQ's capital stack. Regin responded, "We believe on top of this being an accretive transaction... that it is a very safe, secure investment and that we sit in front of the approximately $4 billion in equity that IQHQ has raised since inception."

  • Catherwood inquired about conflict of interest review. Gold stated, "IIP used a very focused and logical methodology... by employing a special committee where the committee members had no interest in IQHQ."

  • William Kirk, MKM Partners, asked about the opportunity cost versus share buybacks and investment flexibility. Gold responded, "We look at our overall cost of capital...we believe this to be a very highly accretive transaction especially when you consider that we are using capital that has been sitting and earning 3%, 4% and now has the opportunity to earn in that average of 14%."

  • Aaron Thomas Grey, Alliance Global Partners, probed the rationale behind diversifying capital away from cannabis and the timing regarding the dividend amid tenant defaults. Gold noted, "We've been strategically evaluating different transactions and opportunities. And this opportunity came about. And it appeared to provide what we were looking for -- a strategic investment that was of size that had the current income and the overall yield that met our -- what we believed our high cost of capital was and is."

  • Alexander David Goldfarb, Piper Sandler, asked if the IQHQ partnership signaled a strategic shift away from cannabis. Smithers responded, "We are still committed to the cannabis industry. We are the leading providers of capital will remain that way. I think we have telegraphed explicitly in our last couple of calls that there's not that many opportunities currently in the cannabis field."

  • Merrill Hadady Ross, Compass Point, asked about the investment structure. Gold explained, "The current cash yield on our -- on the investment is north of 10%."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was mixed to slightly skeptical, with repeated questions on the timing, rationale, and risk of the IQHQ investment and the future focus of IIPR. Several analysts pressed for clarity on investment returns and the company’s commitment to the cannabis sector.

  • Management tone was confident and assertive in prepared remarks and during Q&A, using phrases like "very well thought out, very well researched," "very highly accretive transaction," and "we remain confident with the plan."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts displayed heightened concern over the strategic shift and investment risk, while management maintained a confident and measured stance.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • This quarter marked a significant strategic shift with the first major non-cannabis investment in IQHQ, compared to last quarter's focus on tenant remediation and portfolio optimization within cannabis.

  • Revenue and AFFO both declined 12% sequentially due to tenant defaults, a steeper drop than in the previous quarter, which saw a 6.5% sequential revenue decrease.

  • Analysts were more focused on the strategic rationale and risks of diversification, compared to last quarter’s emphasis on tenant remediation and the cannabis market.

  • Management’s confidence in long-term growth and diversification was more pronounced, while reiterating commitment to the cannabis sector but acknowledging reduced immediate opportunities in that industry.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted the risk from tenant defaults and detailed ongoing legal proceedings to recover assets and rents from 4Front Ventures, Gold Flora, PharmaCann, and TILT Holdings, noting unpredictable timelines due to legal complexity.

  • Regulatory headwinds and competition from illicit and international criminal networks continue to pressure the cannabis industry, with Smithers citing a recent $500 million seizure in California as evidence of the scale of the issue.

  • Analysts expressed concerns about the opportunity cost, risk profile, and strategic implications of the IQHQ investment versus potential share repurchases or further cannabis investments.

Final Takeaway

IIPR management positioned the $270 million IQHQ investment as a high-yield, accretive opportunity that both diversifies earnings and leverages management’s life sciences expertise, while reaffirming commitment to the cannabis sector amid ongoing tenant challenges and legal proceedings. The outlook is shaped by near-term headwinds in cannabis, expectations of a 7% CAGR in that market through 2029, and the strategic flexibility provided by new exposure to the life sciences sector, with management emphasizing prudent capital deployment and risk-adjusted returns for shareholders.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: D-Wave signals accelerated R&D and M&A focus with record $819.3M cash position while scaling quantum AI and Advantage2 deployments
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: D-Wave Quantum Inc. (QBTS) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Alan E. Baratz, President, CEO & Director, highlighted the launch and general availability of the Advantage2 quantum computer, describing it as "our most advanced and performance system" and noting it is "capable of solving computationally complex problems beyond the reach of classical computers." Baratz detailed significant technical advances such as "double coherence time for faster time to solution, a 40% increase in energy scale for higher quality solutions and increased qubit connectivity from 15 to 20 way."
  • Baratz announced a new strategic development initiative focused on advanced cryogenic packaging, leveraging processes at the NASA Jet Propulsion Laboratory to support scaling toward a 100,000-qubit system. He stated, "We have demonstrated end-to-end superconducting interconnect between chips, work that we expect will serve as an important foundation for scaling both our annealing and our gate model systems."
  • The company introduced developer tools for quantum AI, including an open source toolkit integrating D-Wave’s quantum computers with PyTorch, and reported active collaborations with customers like Japan Tobacco and Triumph to build quantum-classical machine learning applications.
  • Baratz noted new and renewing customer engagements with E.ON, GE Vernova, National Quantum Computing Center (UK), Nikon, NTT Data, NTT DOCOMO, Sharp, University of Oxford, and a Fortune 500 aerospace company.
  • A strategic relationship was formed with Yonsei University and Incheon Metropolitan City in South Korea, supporting potential on-site Advantage2 system acquisition.
  • Baratz emphasized D-Wave's cash position: "With the strongest cash position in our company's history, we believe that we are very well positioned to explore M&A activity that will propel our business even further and faster."
  • John M. Markovich, Chief Financial Officer, stated, "Revenue for the second quarter of fiscal 2025 totaled $3.1 million, an increase of about $900,000 or 42% from the second quarter of fiscal 2024."

Outlook

  • Markovich stated, "For the balance of this year, we expect these incremental investments will result in a quarterly non-GAAP OpEx that is approximately 15% higher than our second quarter actual non-GAAP OpEx."
  • Baratz confirmed a focus on M&A as a strategic priority, with plans to "start being able to announce acquisitions this year," though timing remains uncertain.
  • The company aims to accelerate investment in R&D and expand its professional services organization geographically to meet growing demand across commercial and government sectors.

Financial Results

  • Markovich reported, "Bookings for the second quarter totaled $1.3 million, an increase of approximately $600,000 or 92% from the second quarter of 2024."
  • GAAP gross profit for Q2 was $2 million, up $600,000 from the prior year.
  • Non-GAAP gross margin was 71.8%, a slight decrease from the 73.1% in Q2 2024.
  • Net loss for Q2 was $167.3 million, primarily due to $142 million in noncash, nonoperating charges related to warrant liability. Excluding these, adjusted net loss was $25.3 million.
  • Consolidated cash position at quarter-end was $819.3 million, up from $40.9 million a year ago and $304.3 million at the end of Q1, with over $500 million in equity raised during the quarter.

Q&A

  • Craig Andrew Ellis, B. Riley Securities: Asked about M&A strategy and timing. Baratz replied, "We have not disclosed our strategy and plans for M&A other than to say that with $800 million in the bank, it has now become a strategic priority...think about it as really accelerating our R&D and product development activities in a number of key areas..."
  • David Neil Williams, Benchmark: Asked about the cryogenic news and its significance. Baratz explained, "This is key to both scaling our annealing systems as well as developing our gate model systems. And frankly, we made progress in this area much faster than we actually expected it to."
  • Troy Donavon Jensen, Cantor Fitzgerald: Inquired about Advantage2 system deployments. Baratz said, "Advantage2 is a really important system for us...we tried to get this result on our earlier Advantage system, and we were not able to. It took the increased capability of Advantage2...We're talking maybe 6 or 7 Advantage2 systems."
  • William Kingsley Crane, Canaccord Genuity: Asked about U.S. government interest and DARPA's Quantum Benchmarking Initiative. Baratz responded, "The DARPA QBI program is totally focused on gate model. And I think that is a huge mistake...annealing represents, I think, not only the best, but the only quantum approach that can address many of the government's hard computational problems."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed positive sentiment, congratulating the company on technical milestones, product launches, and financial progress, while probing for specifics on M&A, system sales, and customer adoption.
  • Management maintained a confident tone during prepared remarks and Q&A, emphasizing technical achievements, customer traction, and capital strength. Baratz frequently reinforced confidence with statements such as "the future looks very bright."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts appeared more focused on near-term M&A and investment deployment, while management’s tone remained confident but more specific about accelerated investment and expansion.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from general confidence in reaching profitability to a specific commitment to increased operating expenses and M&A as strategic priorities.
  • Key metric changes include a lower quarter-over-quarter revenue due to a large system sale in Q1, but higher bookings and cash balance in Q2.
  • Management’s tone shifted from celebrating quantum supremacy and system sales to highlighting the company’s ability to scale, invest, and pursue acquisitions with its record cash position.
  • Analysts’ focus evolved from customer adoption and technical differentiation in Q1 to capital allocation and execution speed in Q2.

Risks and Concerns

  • Baratz acknowledged deals with large enterprises are "taking longer to close than what we had originally anticipated" due to "complex transaction structures" and "very rigid procurement processes."
  • Markovich noted non-GAAP operating expenses will increase by approximately 15% in the coming quarters.
  • Management discussed the challenge of U.S. government adoption, noting "progress more broadly in the U.S. government...is yes, but slow."
  • Noncash warrant-related charges significantly affected the net loss for the quarter.

Final Takeaway

D-Wave Quantum Inc. delivered technical and commercial progress in Q2 2025, highlighted by the general availability of the Advantage2 system, expanded customer engagements, and a record $819.3 million cash position. Management signaled a shift toward accelerated R&D, expanded professional services, and M&A activity as strategic priorities. While customer demand and bookings are growing, the company acknowledges longer sales cycles with large enterprises and a rising investment profile for the remainder of 2025. The Advantage2 roll-out, quantum AI initiatives, and strengthened balance sheet position D-Wave for further expansion and execution of its aggressive product and market strategy.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Martin Marietta raises 2025 adjusted EBITDA guidance to $2.3B while expanding aggregates portfolio through Quikrete asset exchange
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Martin Marietta Materials, Inc. (MLM) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO C. Howard Nye reported that Martin Marietta delivered record operational and financial results in Q2, achieving the safest six-month start in company history and highlighting the resilience of its business model. Nye announced "we entered into a definitive agreement with Quikrete Holdings for the exchange of certain assets. Specifically, Martin Marietta will receive aggregate operations producing approximately 20 million tons annually in Virginia, Missouri, Kansas and Vancouver, British Columbia, as well as $450 million of cash. In exchange, Quikrete will receive our Midlothian cement plant, related cement terminals and North Texas ready mix concrete assets." Nye emphasized that this strategic move is in line with the SOAR 2025 plan, aiming to shape "a higher-margin enterprise that is increasingly aggregates-led."

  • Nye also stated, "Martin Marietta established new records for the second quarter with key metrics showing gains year-over-year as sustained pricing momentum and effective cost management continue to yield strong results."

  • CFO Michael J. Petro said, "The Building Materials business posted revenues of $1.7 billion, a 2% increase. Gross profit increased 3% to $517 million and gross margin of 30% improved modestly." Petro noted the upward revision in full-year capital expenditures to a range of $820 million to $850 million, citing attractive and opportunistic land purchases.

  • Nye highlighted, "Given our strong first half results and third quarter-to-date shipping trends, we're increasing our full year 2025 adjusted EBITDA guidance to $2.3 billion at the midpoint."

Outlook

  • Martin Marietta increased its full year 2025 adjusted EBITDA guidance to $2.3 billion at the midpoint, reflecting stronger aggregates pricing and contributions from the Premier Magnesia acquisition. Nye explained, "As we progress through the year's second half, our teams remain focused on the key levers within our control: world-class safety, executing our strategic plan, commercial discipline and prudent cost management."

  • Management indicated that infrastructure spending remains resilient, supported by a 10% year-over-year increase in state and local government contract awards, and ongoing legislative efforts signal continued federal momentum.

  • Nye signaled that residential activity is expected to remain subdued in the near term, while nonresidential activity, particularly data centers and energy-related projects, provides medium-term upside.

Financial Results

  • Martin Marietta reported consolidated adjusted EBITDA of $630 million, an 8% increase. Aggregates revenues reached $1.32 billion, up 6%. Aggregates gross profit was $430 million, a 9% improvement, with gross profit per ton at $8.16, up 10%.

  • Magnesia Specialties achieved record quarterly revenues of $90 million and a gross margin of 40%. Cement and Concrete segment revenues decreased 6% to $245 million, while gross profit dropped 25% to $54 million. Asphalt and paving revenues decreased 7% to $228 million, and gross profit declined 8% to $33 million.

  • Full year capital expenditures are now expected at $820 million to $850 million, revised upward from prior guidance.

Q&A

  • Kathryn Thompson, Thompson Research Group: Asked for more color on July trends and volume outlook. Nye responded, "What we saw in July was actually nice double-digit volume up across the enterprise... as I'm looking at volume through July, frankly, we're above the midpoint of the previous guide."

  • Adam Thalhimer, Thompson, Davis: Inquired about confidence behind guidance increase. Nye cited "good, strong first half results through a pretty challenging weather period" and noted "third quarter to date shipment trends... looked awfully good as well."

  • Anthony Pettinari, Citi: Asked about strategic fit and quality of Quikrete assets. Nye emphasized, "It's about 1.3 billion tons... the geographies line up very much with what we were talking about in February of 2021 when we outlined what we wanted to do in SOAR 2025."

  • Steven Fisher, UBS: Asked about lower volume guidance. Nye clarified, "We're being measured and thoughtful on the way that we look at that. Clearly, weather through half 1 was an impediment... If we're just looking through July right now, we would be above the midpoint of the prior guide."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed on volume recovery, guidance confidence, and strategic rationale for asset swaps, displaying a neutral to slightly positive tone, with questions focused on execution and upside potential.

  • Management maintained a confident and measured tone, repeatedly highlighting resilience and strategic discipline. Nye frequently referenced "measured" guidance and strong performance, and Petro projected confidence in continued cost control and M&A activity.

  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone was more assertive regarding guidance increases and strategic asset moves, while analysts maintained a similar focus on operational and market trends.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter saw an upward revision in adjusted EBITDA guidance from $2.25 billion to $2.3 billion at the midpoint. Management’s commentary emphasized stronger aggregates pricing and increased capital expenditures, compared to the prior quarter’s focus on cost management and inventory drawdown.

  • The Quikrete asset exchange marks a notable strategic shift, accelerating the company’s transition toward an aggregates-led portfolio.

  • Analysts' questions shifted from macro uncertainty and M&A pipeline stability last quarter, to detailed execution of strategic initiatives and specific impacts of portfolio reshaping this quarter.

  • Management’s tone has become more confident, particularly around growth initiatives and the durability of end-market demand drivers.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified weather as a recurring headwind impacting volumes, with Nye noting, "weather through half 1 was an impediment."

  • Subdued residential demand persists, with management not projecting a near-term turnaround.

  • Cement and concrete segments experienced margin pressures due to lower operating leverage and higher raw material costs.

  • Analysts probed for clarity on volume guidance, pricing realization under variable weather conditions, and integration of newly acquired assets.

Final Takeaway

Martin Marietta’s Q2 2025 call highlighted record financial results and an upward revision to full year adjusted EBITDA guidance, driven by strong aggregates performance and effective cost management. The company’s strategic focus on transitioning to an aggregates-led portfolio was reinforced by the Quikrete asset exchange, while management signaled confidence in durable infrastructure demand and continued M&A activity. Weather-related volume variability and sectoral headwinds in residential construction remain ongoing challenges, but management’s measured outlook and emphasis on operational resilience underpin the company’s long-term growth narrative.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Dash to the top: DoorDash's impressive Q2 performance fuels $100B GOV pace
data.description:
DoorDash (NASDAQ:DASH) reported another quarter of double-digit marketplace gross order value (GOV) and total orders, contributing to a profit in the second quarter and sales that exceeded Wall Street’s ambitious expectations.

“Quite rare to see a company pacing at a nearly $100B GOV run rate post an across the board acceleration quarter,” Wells Fargo’s Ken Gawrelski said, adding that the company’s guidance for the third quarter suggests continued strength.

The food delivery platform reported a 23% increase in marketplace GOV, 20% increase in total orders, and 25% increase in sales, all of which beat expectations. This led DoorDash (NASDAQ:DASH) to set third quarter marketplace GOV at $24.2B to $24.7B versus $23.8B estimates, and adjusted EBITDA range of $680M to $780M, straddling the $719M estimate.

The company’s ability to deliver “robust” EBITDA and free cash flow growth reflects its acquisition strategy, health of the marketplace, and user engagement with the platform. Evercore ISI’s Mark Mahaney believes this will help the company maintain its 20% revenue growth trajectory “thanks to its market leadership position, excellent execution, and its very large [total addressable market].”

“We believe DASH is likely taking market share in both restaurant and grocery delivery across most of its markets, and our recent Annual Delivery Survey showed DASH being the #1 preference for U.S. grocery delivery for the first time,” Mahaney added.

Despite the ongoing macro uncertainty in the U.S. Wedbush’s Scott Dewitt is “encouraged by the strength implied by the third quarter outlook,” and raised his estimates accordingly.

Dewitt lifted his Q3 GOV and adjusted EBITDA estimates by 3% and 5%, respectively, and price target by 40% on Neutral-rated DoorDash (NASDAQ:DASH).

Mahaney reiterated his Outperform rating but raised his price target by a more generous 50% to $360 by rolling forward his valuation to 2027 based on 32x 2027 EV/EBITDA of $4.8B and 32x EV/FCF per share of $11.48B.

Analysts are largely split on DoorDash (DASH) with Seeking Alpha authors rating the stock as a Hold, but Wall Street analysts giving it a Buy rating. Seeking Alpha’s Quant rating also views DoorDash (DASH) as a Hold with a score of 3.22 out of 5.

Shares are up more than 110% from a year ago, outperforming the S&P 500 by nearly 5x.
